rcomplex 0.1.0
  • Reference
  • Articles
    • Comparative co-expression analysis with rcomplex

Detect co-expression modules in a network

Source: R/modules.R
detect_modules.Rd

Applies community detection to a thresholded co-expression network using the Leiden algorithm, Infomap, or a Stochastic Block Model (SBM).

detect_modules(
  net,
  method = c("leiden", "infomap", "sbm"),
  resolution = 1,
  objective_function = c("CPM", "modularity"),
  n_iterations = 2L,
  nb_trials = 10L,
  seed = NULL,
  consensus_threshold = NULL,
  n_cores = 1L,
  max_consensus_iter = 10L,
  test_k1 = TRUE,
  n_perm_k1 = 100L,
  alpha_k1 = 0.05
)

Arguments

net

Network object from compute_network().

method

Community detection method: "leiden" (default), "infomap", or "sbm".

resolution

Resolution parameter for Leiden (default 1.0). Pass a numeric vector (e.g., seq(0.5, 2.0, by = 0.5)) to run at multiple resolutions and produce a consensus partition via co-classification (Lancichinetti & Fortunato, 2012). Ignored for other methods.

objective_function

Leiden objective: "CPM" (default) or "modularity". Ignored for other methods.

n_iterations

Number of Leiden iterations (default 2). Ignored for other methods.

nb_trials

Number of Infomap attempts; best result is kept (default 10). Ignored for other methods.

seed

Random seed for reproducibility (default NULL).

consensus_threshold

Threshold for consensus mode. NULL (default) uses iterative adaptive thresholding per Jeub et al. (2018): subtracts the per-pair expected co-classification under random assignment and iterates until the partition converges. A numeric value in (0, 1) applies a fixed threshold without iteration (single pass).

n_cores

Number of parallel cores (default 1). Used for mclapply Leiden sweeps on Unix and OpenMP edge scans in C++. Uses fork-based parallelism; avoid combining with active CUDA contexts in the same session.

max_consensus_iter

Maximum number of consensus iterations for adaptive mode (consensus_threshold = NULL). Default 10. Typically converges in 2–5 iterations. Ignored when consensus_threshold is numeric.

test_k1

Logical. Test the null hypothesis K = 1 (no community structure) via permutation of the spectral norm of the excess co-classification matrix. Default TRUE. Only used in adaptive consensus mode.

n_perm_k1

Number of permutations for the K = 1 test. Default 100.

alpha_k1

Significance level for the K = 1 test. Default 0.05.

Value

A list with components:

modules

Named integer vector of module assignments (gene -> module ID)

module_genes

Named list: module ID -> character vector of gene names

n_modules

Number of modules detected

modularity

Modularity score of the partition

graph

The igraph graph object used for community detection

method

Method used

params

List of parameters used

When resolution is a vector, the output also includes:

resolution_scan

Data frame with columns resolution, n_modules, modularity, ari_next (Adjusted Rand Index with the next resolution; NA for the last), and expected_coclassification (per-resolution expected scalar).

k1_test

When test_k1 = TRUE, a list with components: lambda_obs (observed spectral norm), lambda_null (null distribution), p_value, and has_structure.

The params list includes n_consensus_iterations (number of iterations until convergence; 0 for fixed threshold).

Details

The adaptive consensus path uses sparse co-classification restricted to the original network's edge set, reducing memory from O(N^2) to O(|E|).

Methods

leiden

Modularity or CPM optimization with guaranteed well-connected communities (Traag et al., 2019). Resolution parameter controls module granularity.

infomap

Flow-based method that compresses the description of random walks on the network (Rosvall & Bergstrom, 2008). No resolution parameter; naturally handles weighted networks.

sbm

Gaussian Stochastic Block Model fit by variational EM (requires the sbm package). Number of blocks is selected automatically via the Integrated Classification Likelihood (ICL). Can detect both assortative and non-assortative structure.

References

Traag, V. A., Waltman, L. & van Eck, N. J. (2019). From Louvain to Leiden: guaranteeing well-connected communities. Scientific Reports, 9, 5233. doi:10.1038/s41598-019-41695-z

Rosvall, M. & Bergstrom, C. T. (2008). Maps of random walks on complex networks reveal community structure. PNAS, 105(4), 1118–1123. doi:10.1073/pnas.0706851105

Lancichinetti, A. & Fortunato, S. (2012). Consensus clustering in complex networks. Scientific Reports, 2, 336. doi:10.1038/srep00336

Jeub, L. G. S., Sporns, O. & Fortunato, S. (2018). Multiresolution consensus clustering in networks. Scientific Reports, 8, 3259. doi:10.1038/s41598-018-21352-7

Senbabaoglu, Y. et al. (2014). Critical limitations of consensus clustering in class discovery. Scientific Reports, 4, 6207. doi:10.1038/srep06207

Examples

if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{
# Single resolution
mods <- detect_modules(net, method = "leiden", resolution = 1.0)
table(mods$modules)  # module sizes

# Multi-resolution consensus (Jeub et al. 2018)
mods_consensus <- detect_modules(net, resolution = c(0.5, 1.0, 2.0),
                                 n_cores = 4L)
} # }
